name: Selenium (as Selenomethionine)
historicalUse: Selenium as an element was not known to classical medicine, but selenium-rich foods including garlic, onion, and grain preparations from selenium-rich soils appear throughout Arabic and Mediterranean dietary medicine. Rambam's dietary recommendations emphasizing garlic and allium preparations for vitality and disease prevention align with modern understanding of selenium's role in glutathione peroxidase function and antioxidant defense.
classicalSources: Empirical parallels in classical garlic and allium therapeutics — Ibn Sina, Maimonides
modernEvidence: Selenium is an essential trace mineral and obligate component of 25 selenoprotein enzymes, including all five glutathione peroxidases (GPx), thioredoxin reductase, and iodothyronine deiodinases (thyroid hormone activation). SelenoExcell® (high-selenium yeast, organic selenomethionine) has GRAS status and is the form with the strongest evidence for bioavailability and cancer prevention research. The SELECT and NPC trials established selenium's role in cellular defense, though outcomes varied by baseline selenium status.
mechanisms: Glutathione peroxidase cofactor — essential for all 5 GPx enzymes that neutralize peroxides
Thioredoxin reductase activation — regenerates thioredoxin for DNA synthesis and redox balance
Thyroid hormone activation — iodothyronine deiodinase converts T4 to active T3
p53 pathway support — selenium-dependent selenoprotein P supports tumor suppressor function
Sperm motility — selenoprotein in sperm mitochondrial capsule essential for male fertility
benefits: Supports antioxidant defense via glutathione peroxidase activation
Contributes to cellular DNA protection and repair
safetyProfile: Safe at 100–200mcg/day. Selenomethionine form is well absorbed and well tolerated. Selenium toxicity (selenosis) occurs at chronic intakes >400mcg/day — characterized by garlic breath, hair loss, nail changes. SelenoExcell® organic form has superior safety to inorganic selenate/selenite.
contraindications: Do not exceed 400mcg/day from all sources — toxicity risk
Autoimmune thyroid disease — selenium modulates thyroid immunity; consult physician
drugInteractions: Chemotherapy (cisplatin) — selenium may reduce nephrotoxicity; discuss with oncologist
Anticoagulants — high doses may have mild antiplatelet activity
Thyroid medications — selenium affects thyroid hormone metabolism; monitor TSH
physicianNotes: Assess baseline selenium status if clinically relevant (RBC selenium or plasma selenium). US soil selenium is variable — deficiency more common in certain geographic regions. SelenoExcell® preferred over sodium selenite (inorganic) for tolerability. In autoimmune thyroiditis (Hashimoto's), selenium 200mcg/day has demonstrated reduction in anti-TPO antibodies in clinical trials.
patientGuidance: Do not take additional selenium supplements if you are already taking a selenium-containing multivitamin — total daily intake from all sources should stay under 400mcg. Brazil nuts are also very high in selenium — limit intake if supplementing.
